conditionDescriptions: chronic kidney disease with acute kidney injury risk
contraindications.clinicalRationale: NSAIDs are nephrotoxic and can cause acute kidney injury, particularly in patients with CKD receiving ACE inhibitors or ARBs. They impair kidney potassium excretion and can contribute to hyperkalemia.
contraindications.alternativeConsiderations: discontinue NSAIDs and consider alternative pain management
contraindications.sourceSnippet: History of the use of over-the-counter nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), supplements, and herbal treatments should be pursued, and patients should be counseled to discontinue these remedies if present.

contraindications.clinicalRationale: NSAIDs are identified as nephrotoxic drugs that should be investigated and discontinued when there is >30% increase in serum creatinine after initiating ACE inhibitors or ARBs in diabetic CKD patients.
contraindications.riskDescription: acute kidney injury with creatinine rise >30%
contraindications.alternativeConsiderations: investigate for nephrotoxic drug use and discontinue NSAIDs
contraindications.sourceSnippet: Greater than 30% increase in creatinine: review for causes of AKI, correct volume depletion, reassess concomitant medications (e.g., diuretics, NSAIDs), consider renal artery stenosis.

warnings.warningText: NSAIDs interfere with kidney potassium excretion and can contribute to hyperkalemia in patients receiving ACE inhibitors or ARBs
warnings.affectedPopulation: patients with CKD receiving ACE inhibitors or ARBs
warnings.actionRequired: Monitor serum creatinine and potassium within 2-4 weeks after starting or changing dose of ACEi or ARB. Review concurrent NSAID use and discontinue if hyperkalemia develops.
